result:
# On the Particular Pleasure of Unfinished Things

There's a half-built stone wall in a field I once walked past in Ireland, and I think about it more often than seems reasonable. It wasn't a ruin—nothing that ambitious. It was just a wall someone had started and not finished. Maybe they ran out of stones, or time, or the will to keep stacking. The wall ran for about forty feet and then simply stopped, mid-row, the last stone slightly askew as if placed in a hurry. Beyond it, the field continued, undivided, indifferent to whatever boundary had been imagined for it.

I don't know why I think about this wall. I think it's because finished things have a way of closing themselves off from you. A completed building tells you what it is. A poem with its final line in place becomes a thing you can either understand or fail to understand, but in either case it has stopped becoming. The half-wall, though—the half-wall is still a question. It's still asking what it wanted to be. And because it never got there, it stays alive in a way that the finished walls around it do not.

I suspect this is also why I love rough drafts more than published books, why I'd rather see a sketch than a finished painting, why I sometimes prefer the demos on the back of an album to the polished tracks at the front. There's something honest about a thing that hasn't yet learned to lie smoothly about itself. A draft is still trying. It hasn't yet figured out how to perform being a draft. The marginalia and the cross-outs are evidence of a mind that was, very recently, alive in a particular spot, doing the work of choosing one word over another. The finished version smooths over all of that. It tells you what was chosen, but not what was rejected, and so much of what makes thinking interesting is what it almost said but didn't.

This is probably a defense of my own incompleteness, if I'm honest. Most of us prefer the aesthetic theories that make our shortcomings into virtues. I have a notebook somewhere with about thirty essay openings and maybe three endings. I've been telling myself for years that this is just how it goes, that the openings are the interesting part anyway, that the endings are where writers settle for whatever they can get. There may be some truth in this, but mostly it's a story I tell myself to feel better about leaving things undone. The stone wall doesn't need that story. The stone wall was abandoned by someone with their own reasons, and it doesn't owe me an aesthetics.

But I keep coming back to it anyway, because there's something true in there even if I'm using it for my own purposes. Consider: most of human life is unfinished. Most conversations end without resolution. Most relationships fade without explicit conclusion. Most thoughts are never quite worked out. We talk about life as if it had a structure—beginnings, middles, endings—but really it has the structure of a Tuesday afternoon, which is to say no structure at all, just one thing after another until the things stop. And we are taught, mostly, to be ashamed of this. To finish things. To bring projects to completion. To resolve arguments. To tie up loose ends. As if the loose ends were the failure rather than the truth.

I'm not arguing against finishing things. I like books with last pages. I appreciate a meal with a dessert. I want, when I die, to have done a few things all the way through. But I think we underrate the dignity of the incomplete, the partial, the abandoned, the merely begun. A life is not less of a life for being a heap of half-walls. It might, in fact, be more honest about what a life actually is.

---

I want to write about something else now, but I notice that I've set myself the task of writing 2500 words, and there's already a small voice in me asking what the shape of this should be. Should it have a theme? Should the pieces connect? Should I be building toward something? I'm going to ignore that voice as much as I can, because the prompt was to write freely, and free writing that's secretly trying to structure itself is just regular writing in a disguise.

So let me tell you about a man I saw at a train station once. This was years ago, in a city I don't remember, and the man was eating a sandwich while reading a book, which is not unusual. What was unusual was that he was crying. Not sobbing—just letting tears run down his face while he ate and read, calmly, as if the crying were happening to someone else. He'd take a bite, turn a page, wipe his cheek absently, take another bite. I watched him for maybe two minutes before my train came. I have wondered ever since what the book was. I've never been able to imagine a book that would make me cry like that in public while eating a sandwich. Either the book was tremendous or the man was already crying about something else and had brought the book and the sandwich as a kind of cover, a way to be in public while privately falling apart.

I think about him sometimes when I read books that are supposed to be sad. The books almost never make me cry. I think the trick of crying-while-reading is not in the book at all—it's in the reader, in some particular alignment of their life with whatever the book happens to be saying. The book is just the occasion. Anything could be the occasion. The man at the train station might have been brought to tears by a tax form if he'd been carrying one. The book gets credit because it happens to be there.

This is a general principle I believe in: most of what we think causes our feelings is actually just the thing we happened to be looking at when the feeling arrived. We give the credit to the trigger because we want to feel like we understand our own emotional lives. But emotional life is much weirder than that. It rises and falls for reasons we can't track, and then attaches itself to whatever's nearby, the way a vine attaches to a fence. The fence didn't cause the vine. The fence was just there.

---

I've been thinking lately about the strange ethics of attention. There's so much I could be paying attention to right now—the news, the weather, the various small disasters and small triumphs happening to people I know. And what I'm doing instead is writing about a half-wall and a crying man and the nature of incompleteness. Is this a moral failure? Probably not, in any large sense. But I notice that I have to defend it to myself, that there's some little internal accountant who keeps tallying up what I might be doing instead of whatever I am doing, and the score is usually unfavorable.

I suspect everyone has this accountant. I suspect it's not the same as a conscience, which has to do with right and wrong, and is at least sometimes useful. The accountant is just about utility. It wants you to be productive. It wants every minute to be in service of some larger goal. It would, I think, be horrified by the idea of just sitting on a bench in a park and looking at a tree for an hour.

But sitting on a bench in a park and looking at a tree for an hour might be, in some long-term cosmic sense, exactly the right use of an hour. The tree is one of the most remarkable things in the universe. It is converting sunlight into matter through a process so chemically elegant that we still don't fully understand all of its mechanisms. It has been alive longer than you have, and it is doing nothing—nothing!—but standing there and turning light into wood. To ignore the tree in favor of answering emails is, on some level, a kind of madness. But it's a madness we've all agreed to, and so the tree-ignorer is considered productive and the tree-watcher is considered to be wasting his time.

I'm not trying to romanticize idleness. I know that someone has to fix the plumbing and harvest the wheat and pull the children out of the burning building. I'm just saying that the accountant in our heads is overconfident. He doesn't actually know what the best use of an hour is. He's just running a simple algorithm that says: more output, more output, more output. He doesn't have the data to know whether your life is going well or badly. He's just one little voice among many, and we've made him the boss for reasons that probably have more to do with capitalism than with human flourishing.

---

What else. I want to say something about silence, because I've been thinking about it. There are different kinds of silence and we tend to lump them all together, which is a mistake. There's the silence of a room when no one is in it, which is just the absence of speech. There's the silence between two people who have run out of things to say, which is awkward and freighted. There's the silence of two people who don't need to say anything, which is one of the rarest and most valuable things in human experience. There's the silence after a piece of music ends, before the applause starts, which is sometimes the most important part of the music. There's the silence of someone choosing not to answer a question, which can be more eloquent than any answer. There's the silence of grief, which is its own country. There's the silence before a storm, which is a quality of the air itself, not of any human ear.

We use one word for all of these. This seems insufficient. The Inuit famously don't actually have all those words for snow, but the principle is right: when something matters to your life, you need a vocabulary for it. We have, in modern life, very little vocabulary for silence, because we have so little of it. We've filled our environments with noise—traffic, music, talking, the hum of machines—and so we don't need ways to distinguish between the silences, because we hardly ever encounter one.

This is probably a real loss. Not because silence is intrinsically virtuous—it isn't—but because some kinds of thinking only happen in some kinds of silence, and if you never have access to those silences, you never have access to those kinds of thinking. There are thoughts that can only form in a quiet room. There are realizations that only arrive when nothing is competing for your attention. The modern world is, in some real sense, anti-thought, because it never shuts up for long enough for thought to settle.

I notice I'm being preachy. I don't mean to be. I have not, myself, mastered silence. I have my phone in my pocket like everyone else, and I check it more often than I'd like to admit. I'm not standing on a mountain telling you how to live. I'm just noticing, from down here in the noise, that there's something we're missing, and I don't know exactly what to do about it.

---

A confession: I find it surprisingly hard to write 2500 words about nothing in particular. I always imagined that if you gave me unlimited freedom and asked me to fill some pages, I would be a fountain. Words would come pouring out. But what I'm discovering is that constraints are a lot of what makes writing work. When you're trying to write about a specific thing, the specific thing tells you what you need to say. It pushes back against you. It says, "No, not that, that's not quite right." Without a specific thing to push back against, you're just stacking sentences, and the sentences don't know what they want to do.

This is a useful thing to discover. I will try to remember it the next time I'm complaining about the constraints of a particular project. The constraints are doing more work than I realize. If I take them away, I won't suddenly be free—I'll just be lost.

There's a wider lesson here, maybe, about freedom in general. We tend to imagine freedom as the absence of restriction: you're free to the extent that no one is telling you what to do. But total absence of restriction is actually a kind of paralysis. If anyone could do anything, no one would know what to do. Most of what we call freedom is really the freedom to choose within a fairly narrow set of options that has been pre-curated by our circumstances. And those circumstances, for all their constraints, are usually what makes meaningful action possible. The chess player isn't constrained by the rules of chess—the chess player is enabled by them. Without the rules, there'd be no game to play.

I think this is what people miss when they imagine that they'd be happiest if they had infinite money, or no job, or no responsibilities. They imagine that they're being held back by their constraints, and that without the constraints, the real them would emerge and flourish. But what would actually emerge, in many cases, is a person with no idea what to do with their day. The constraints were doing the work of giving the day a shape.

---

I should probably try to wrap this up. I've been writing for a while now and I'm aware that I've been circling a few themes—incompleteness, attention, silence, constraint—without ever quite landing on any of them. This feels appropriate to the prompt. To write freely is, I think, to allow yourself to circle, to not insist on a destination. The destination is where the structure comes in, and the structure is what we said we'd leave out this time.

If there's something all of this has in common, it might be a suspicion of the official version of things. The official version says: finish your work, pay attention to the important stuff, fill the silences, maximize your freedom by removing constraints. And what I've been pushing against, in my circling way, is the sense that the official version is missing most of what makes life livable. The half-wall in the field is not a failure of wall-building—it's a small monument to whatever the wall-builder was actually thinking about that afternoon, which we'll never know. The man crying at the train station was doing something more honest than most of us manage in public. The silence in a quiet room is a resource we are squandering. The constraints we complain about are mostly the reason we can do anything at all.

These are not new ideas. I haven't said anything here that hasn't been said better by other people. But there's a particular value, I think, in working things out in your own words, even if the things have already been worked out elsewhere. The thinking is the point, not the conclusion. The walk is the point, not the destination. The half-wall in the field is the point, not the field it failed to enclose.

I'm going to stop now, not because I've finished, but because I've reached the agreed-upon length. There's a difference between those two things, and I'd like to honor it. The wall stopped because the builder stopped. That was reason enough.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
